program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: Halfway There -- Ten Lessons, One Traveler
lesson.menu_desc: The checkpoint: nine expressions, nine structures, and a whole day in English from the check-in counter to the last tram -- no new grammar, just proof -- 37 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- Halfway There: Progress Check and Fluency Review
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 10 -- Halfway There: Progress Check and Fluency Review

listenings.text: Good morning, ladies and gentlemen. This is the first boarding announcement for the flight to Amsterdam, now boarding at gate twenty-two. Please have your boarding pass and your passport ready in your hand, and not at the bottom of your bag. We are going to board in groups, and I am going to say this only once, so please listen. First: families with small children, and passengers who need a little more time. If that is you, you should come to the desk now. Second: rows twenty to thirty-five, at the back of the aircraft. Third: rows one to nineteen. Please wait for your row. If you come early, you will only stand in the line longer, and the line is already crowded. Now, three things about your bags, and they are important. One: your carry-on goes in the overhead bin, and the small bag goes under the seat in front of you. This aircraft is full today, so there is not much space up there. If the bins are full, we might have to check your carry-on here at the gate. That is free, but you should take your medicine, your documents and your phone out of it first, because you will not see that bag again until Amsterdam. Two: a lot of passengers today are travelling with two carry-on bags, and the limit is one. Please do not make us stop the line. Three: if your bag is damaged when it arrives, do not go home. You should go to the desk in the baggage hall in Amsterdam before you leave the airport, because after you leave, we cannot help you. Our flight time today is two hours and fifty minutes. The weather in Amsterdam is nine degrees and grey, so I would rather be here. Thank you very much, and welcome aboard.

Excuse me. Is this the line for Amsterdam? Thank you. My name is Yuko. May I stand here with you? Twenty minutes is a long time at my age. I am seventy-one. I know, I know. Everybody says I do not look seventy-one, and I always give them the same answer: it depends on the day! Can I ask you something? Your English is good. Where did you learn it? ... Ten lessons. Ten lessons! Then listen to me, because your story is almost my story. I am from Osaka, and I started English when I was sixty-six. Sixty-six! At school I learned nothing. I used to read a little, once in a while, but I never spoke. Not one sentence, for fifty years. And then I retired, and my children said: Mother, we are busy, we have our own lives, go with your sister. And I was angry. I was angry for one whole week! And then I was not angry any more, because they were right. My first trip alone was three days in Seoul, and I cried in the hotel on the first night. In my opinion that is completely normal, and nobody tells you that part. But on the third day I ordered my own dinner. I asked the waiter what he would recommend, he told me, and I understood him. Such a small thing. It is a piece of cake now. That night it was the biggest thing in the world. Now I go somewhere twice a year. And I still make mistakes, of course. Yesterday I said something wrong to a taxi driver here in Lisbon, and he laughed. And I laughed too! I would rather laugh with him than say nothing at all. That is the whole secret, and it took me sixty-six years to learn it. People ask me, Yuko, at seventy-one, why? It is too late. I disagree. It is worth it, every single time. I am looking forward to Amsterdam. I have never been. Look, they are calling our rows. Come on.

paras: It is nine in the morning at the Lisbon airport, and Rosângela is standing at the check-in counter for the flight to Amsterdam. Her husband is sitting on a bench behind her with the coffee, and he is not worried. He does not speak English -- not one word -- and this morning he does not need to. Ten lessons ago, in a room in São Paulo, she said one sentence out loud to her teacher: "My son does everything for me." That was the problem, and she was the one who named it. Today her son is at home, and he does not know that his mother is at this counter. Once in a while she still thinks about the customs officer in Paris, four years ago: a man who asked her one simple question, the twenty seconds of silence that came after it, and her son, who answered for her. That was the old Rosângela. This one is holding her own passport.

"Good morning. I would rather have a window seat, if you have one," she says. The agent looks at the screen and asks how many bags she is checking. One, for two people. He puts it on the scale: twenty-four kilos. "I am afraid there is a small problem. The limit is twenty-three, so you are one kilo over." Four years ago, this is the exact moment she would look for her son. She does not look at the bench. "What could I do?" she asks. "You could pay the excess baggage fee, or you could move one kilo into your carry-on." "How much is the fee?" "Sixty euros." She does not think for very long. "In my opinion that is a lot of money for one kilo. I will move it." She opens the bag on the floor, in front of the whole line, and she moves two books into her carry-on. Thirty seconds. "Twenty-two point eight," he says. "Perfect." Two books, thirty seconds, and sixty euros still in her pocket. It is a piece of cake -- and four years ago it was a wall.

At the gate she calls her son, and she tells him everything in Portuguese, because he is her son and she is excited: the counter, the window seat, the twenty-four kilos, the two books, the sixty euros she did not pay. He is quiet for a second. Then: "Mom... you did all of that alone?" Ten lessons ago it was he who said to her: you need to start going out without me. He was right, and he was also a little afraid of being right, and she knew it. "I used to need you at every counter," she says. "Now I need you for the photographs." He laughs, and then he says: "Mom, you were lucky." "I disagree," she says. "I was not lucky. I was ready." Some days the words come easily and some days they hide -- it depends on the day, and she has stopped fighting that. But it is worth it. Next month she is sixty-nine years old, and she is looking forward to Amsterdam. Ten more lessons after this one. And then Paris -- where there is a customs officer, somewhere, who is going to ask her one simple question.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Rosângela has a problem with her bag at the airport, and she pays sixty euros to solve it quickly.
Ten lessons changed who solves the problem: the same woman who needed her son in Paris now handles the counter, the bag and the fee alone -- and she says she was ready, not lucky.
Rosângela's son is angry because she travelled to Amsterdam without telling him first.

items: Rosângela's husband does not speak English, and this morning at the counter he does not need to.
"He does not speak English -- not one word -- and this morning he does not need to." She is the one at the counter.
The bag is one kilo over the limit, so Rosângela looks for her son, like she did in Paris.
That is exactly what the old Rosângela would do. This one does not look at the bench: she asks "What could I do?" and she solves it herself.
Rosângela pays the sixty euro fee, because it is faster than opening the bag.
She says "In my opinion that is a lot of money for one kilo. I will move it", and she moves two books into her carry-on in thirty seconds.
Ten lessons ago it was her son who told her that she needed to start going out without him.
"Ten lessons ago it was he who said to her: you need to start going out without me." He was right, and he was also a little afraid of being right.
When her son says she was lucky, Rosângela agrees with him.
She disagrees: "I was not lucky. I was ready."

items.situation: Station 1, the check-in counter in Lisbon. You booked the flight two weeks ago and you want a window seat. Open the conversation.
tips: Good morning. I booked this flight two weeks ago, and I would like a window seat, please.
Good morning. Two for Amsterdam. I would rather have a window seat, if you have one.

items.situation: He tells you the bag is one kilo over the limit. You do not want to pay sixty euros. Say what you are going to do about it -- you decided one second ago.
tips: I will move one kilo into my carry-on.
Sixty euros for one kilo? In my opinion that is too much. I will open the bag.

items.situation: Station 2, breakfast at the hotel. The museum opens at ten and it is eight now. Tell your husband the plan you made last night, and one thing you decide right now.
tips: We are going to take the tram at nine thirty. I will get a coffee first.
We have a few hours before it opens. We are going to walk, and I will buy the tickets on my phone now.

items.situation: Station 3, the restaurant. You want to know what the kitchen does best. Ask -- and use a superlative.
tips: What is the most popular dish tonight?
What is the best thing on the menu, in your opinion?

items.situation: The bill has a bottle of wine on it. You drank sparkling water. Say it, calmly, at the table.
tips: There seems to be a problem with the bill. We did not order this wine.
Excuse me. I think we were overcharged -- this bottle is not ours.

items.situation: Station 4, the museum. A tourist asks you what she should see in one afternoon. Give her advice, and give her your own opinion.
tips: You should start upstairs. In my opinion it is the best room in the building.
It depends on your time. If you only have one hour, you should see the top floor -- it is worth it.

items.situation: Your husband asks if the nine euro guided tour is a good idea. He would rather walk alone. Say what you think, and disagree with him politely.
tips: I disagree. It is nine euros, but it is worth it.
I would rather take the tour. In my opinion we understand nothing without it.

items.situation: Station 5. Somebody at the gate asks how often you travel to Europe, and how you learned your English. Answer both, in full sentences.
tips: We come once in a while -- about once a year. And I started English ten lessons ago.
Once in a while, yes. I used to need my son at every counter. Now I do it myself.
